speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, you were speaking of your arrival at Schindler's factory, first seeing the barracks. And then you said the next morning, you were sent-- would you tell us about that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Assigned to work. As you know that Schindler took over a Jewish factory that was producing pots and pans before the war. How he took over the factory, later on we found out, he was advised by some Jewish people when he came to Poland looking, seeking fortunes. And he was advised to take over this factory because this was a good item for the black market. And he can make a lot of money. So we were working over there. And we were assigned to work at painting and producing. This is not the one process, you just paint. There's a whole series of processes before you make the finished product. I was assigned first-- first as a helper to the painter. However, the manager noticed that i was very good with my hands. And I was assigned to be a painter. And the painter also-- there was white paint. And there was the red paint. The red paint was more complicated. But when I was assigned to do something, I tried my best. And I succeeded. I managed to do it because, by doing so, I noticed and I learned quickly, I could avoid many unpleasant things from the managers and other things. So they were pleased with my work. And I had a-- a little bit more peace and quiet doing my job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the managers?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time was a Polish manager.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there was also a Jewish man. You know, they were always on top. You had all kinds of managers. But of course, there was Nazis, professionals.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. You know, you needed all kinds. How to start, to make from nothing, to create a shape and-- and to make it-- and then-- the process was a long process. I was very curious. Even over there, I was very curious how the things are done. And again, I was very obedient, following the orders. And by doing so, I did avoid a lot of unpleasant things, even from the Nazis or the managers in-- in the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the Jews treated by the managers?

speaker: interviewee
text: It depends. Some were, really, there was no problem. They were just doing their work. And it was OK. But if a Nazi wanted to find something, they always managed to find something to make-- to cause problem. And in fact I had-- I myself, the quiet one, the bashful one-- I was in big-- in big trouble one day in Schindler's factory because, like I mentioned before, the Nazis never ceased. They always were looking and finding something to degrade use. One day they announced there will be no work, so we always lived in-- in fear. So we thought that we'll be deported, sent it away somewheres to the death camps. However, the reason for not going to work was different, rather simple, because the Nazis brought full trucks of the clothing, the striped uniforms-- maybe somebody who saw the movie or some books-- so they took everything away from us. And the only outfits we were given were the striped uniforms. Uniforms for the woman was one piece dress. The men had a pair of pants and a jacket and even a hat to make you look more like not normal people. When they gave out those uniforms, and I brought the dress to my barrack, what I was shocked because they brought the dresses one size fits all. And yet I was still a teenager. And I was a very petite girl. And I looked at it. It was huge.

speaker: interviewee
text: And without thinking-- you know, it was almost like rebelling. What did they give me? I went to the lady who was in charge of the barrack-- because we did have in our possessions zero, nothing-- and I asked the lady if she has, by any chance, a pair of scissors and a needle. She looked at me because we were, the whole day, very nervous, tense, not knowing what is gonna be during the day. She gave me the scissors and a needle. I went to my barrack. And I quickly went to work, not asking, not knowing. This was my feeling, that I need to do something in order I can function.

speaker: interviewee
text: I made the dress very short. I made the sleeves fitted. I made my waistline. I even fixed a belt because I had a lot, a lot of extra fabric. If I didn't have thread-- I want you to know-- from the extra fabric, I was unraveling the thread. And this was my sewing thread. The only sad story came a few hours later in the evening, during the roll call. During the roll call, when the Nazi lady-- it was specially one-- she was counting us. And she noticed that I the only girl among the 300 women look different. And she pointed with her finger for me to step forward. So I followed the order. When I came in front of her she, with her open fist, hit my face so hard that I felt my head like an empty box and some ringings in my ears. The rest I don't remember, because right there, on the spot, she gave the order to put me into isolation. And the next morning, I was supposed to be sent back to Plaszow for execution, because this was sabotage, what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Altering the uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. You know, this was against what the Nazis wanted to do. And while I was in this isolation, in the dark room, this quickly came Oskar Schindler's attention, what happened at the roll call. How he did it, what he did, it doesn't matter. How he bribed the SS woman, because a few hours later I was released from this isolation and was told by a Jewish policeman, try to undo the dress as much as possible and to report to work as usual. And so I did. The next morning I was at work. Of course, you know, I-- despite of everything, I looked different because, once my dress was shortened, it was short. And I was at my work bench working, painting. And of course Schindler used to see me, almost-- whenever he would come to the factory. We felt, seeing Schindler walking through the factory, there is somebody who has some compassion, somebody who cares. And we felt like a god-like person is walking through the factory. We never-- I never had a conversation or spoke to him. He came many times very close in looking how I was painting. Sometimes we could almost figure out, like, he would say, just keep working. And he would walk from place to place.

speaker: interviewer
text: You heard him say that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We almost could hear, yes. Just working. Yes, I could figure this out because he never did, you know, come into [INAUDIBLE] anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were 17, 18 years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. I started out 15. And this was in 1940. This is 1943, we are at Schindler's factory. So I am already 18. You must remember that, in my young life, I spent over five years in concentration camps-- five, five and a half years. My best years, in different camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now, you look like you're a size two or four.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I am not.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, you're small.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I'm not so small.

speaker: interviewer
text: You must have been a very small, young teenager.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was always a petite girl. Yes, I was. And while we were working over there, I had another incidence which was very rare, unusual. As you know from the books and when you saw the movie, how Schindler was entertaining the highest Nazis, the murderers. And by entertaining them, bribing them with diamonds, gold, the ladies and everything, he achieved this impossible, by doing those things. One day I was called-- not called, tapped on my shoulder by one of the managers-- and told-- ordered-- to follow him. And so I did. And he told me to get washed the best way I could. And he took me up to Schindler's apartment, where there was a party to be in the evening. And I-- when I was brought up, the only Jewish girl over there, and they-- quickly, in the kitchen, whoever the manager was, they put on me a beautiful white apron. And I was working all evening over there as helping in the kitchen. However, this is something unforgettable because I saw it with my own eyes. I witnessed. After midnight, after the dinner, Schindler opened the huge doors with another Nazi man. He was a engineer in Schindler's factory. They opened the huge door from the ballroom into the kitchen. And they ordered all the help from the kitchen to enter the ballroom, like to get the thank you or applause from those Nazis for good service or for good food. And I could not believe what I saw. Number one, when you entered the ballroom, the-- the-- the chandeliers. The velvets. You couldn't-- you were blinded. And then the music. And you saw the dozen of woman drinking with the Nazis. They were having the big party. And I was among the other group from the kitchen, the only Jewish girl. I saw, with my eyes, I witnessed a party like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: He had these parties all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had, very often.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this apartment was in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was, you know, a special building when-- when you entered the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. Separate from the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The factory was a building by itself. And this was, you know, where offices were and where the-- his apartment and the ballroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you continued to work in the Schindler's factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were working over there until mid-summer of 1944, when the-- when the Russian forces were coming forward. And most of the camps all around were liquidated. And Schindler's factory was also liquidated.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there came the order, you know. No more work. No more nothing. And they rounded up us again, put on open trucks, and we were brought back to Plaszow. And in Plaszow I had, again, an unbelievable experience. I was not the only one. But I am here with you speaking about my own experience. When we were brought back to Plaszow, when I was assigned with other women-- whole groups. There were men working and women working. And we were assigned to reopen the mass graves, carrying the remains that they were buried-- some for longer time and some only recent-- we were carrying the remains to a certain square where there were wooden boards. And we were throwing the remains over there. And the Nazi ordered-- Jews were ordered to pour gasoline over it and burn this to ashes because we know for a fact, the Nazis tried to erase as much evidence as possible what was going on in Plaszow. However, they couldn't succeed. You cannot wipe out all the evidence, no matter how hard you try. Number one-- they did not succeed because there were many things, many signs afterwards. You could see what was going on. But the most important thing-- there were survivors, people who witnessed, who were there, who could attest what we saw, what the Nazis did to our people.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said, besides the survivors, there were certain things they couldn't wipe out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. For instance, you know, do you think they could-- the barracks, there were everywhere people buried. So there were signs, you know, what was going on over there. They tried to erase some barracks. They tried to erase the place where there were the hangings done. There were many things. There were-- there was 24 hours a day going on all kind of murdering in all kind of shapes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you must forget. Nobody exactly knows. But there were over 20, and some say even 30,000 people were murdered in Plaszow, although there were no ovens-- only by killing, hanging, shooting, all kind of things. And what do you do with those people? They were buried in the mass graves. So there was always evidence left all around.

speaker: interviewee
text: And number one-- I-- if you do not realize-- Plaszow was not somewheres in a desert. We were in the middle of civilization where people lived all around. They heard our cries. They saw the smoke. And they saw everything what was going on. The only complaint the Polish people had-- that the stench from the burning flesh or they did not like, for instance, you know, like sometimes the trains, where they were planning to go on vacation yet the railroad tracks were busy with the cattle cars going and coming, because they was from Plaszow. Constantly, people were sended-- sended to some other death camps. And people were brought from different places to Plaszow. So there was like a death factory going back and forth. So some Polish people had complaints that we were disturbing their peace and comfort. And finally, after being a few weeks in Plaszow-- as you know when you heard about Schindler's list-- I really don't know. It is only a miracle how they did not remove the names from this list of my sister and my name, because we were right away on the Schindler's list. In fact, the number on the list-- numbers seven and eight-- are the numbers of my sister and myself. And after the Schindler's list, what was going on, I could not tell you how the list, who made the list. The fact was, when the day came, we were loaded into cattle cars. And we heard rumors only that the 300-- the selected 300 women, Schindler's women-- we will bypass Auschwitz. And we will be traveling directly to his newly established factory in Czechoslovakia in Brunnlitz. However, things turned out differently. Once we were in the cattle cars, we arrived in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- a couple of things I'd like to check, because we're almost at the end of this tape. You mentioned, when they liquidated the Krakow ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and your parents were killed then, did you know it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found-- we-- my sister and I-- we found out maybe a day or two later, because when they selected a group of men to clean the ghetto, and they were loading people on the open trucks, whatever, bringing them from the ghetto to Plaszow, burying them-- to be buried in the mass graves, one of the men who worked over there, he came up and he told us that he saw the bodies of my both parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: About what was the-- the Jewish prisoner population at Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot be completely sure. But there were times we were over 20,000 people in Plaszow. But the numbers usually varied. There was many times much more. The number was greater. And sometime was lower. It depend how many people were deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: But people were constantly coming in?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were brought by force. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they all-- were they all Polish Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Plaszow, they brought people from Hungary. They brought people from other southern European countries.

speaker: interviewer
text: All--

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the Hungarian people. But they were brought from Greece, from somewhere else, to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: All Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm talking about Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you were-- you were telling me, now, after this terrible time, again, in Plaszow, you were-- you thought you were going directly to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what we heard. However, things worked out differently because we were brought to Auschwitz in the cattle cars. And Auschwitz is the most--

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe, first, the-- the transport, the cattle cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were packed in the cars. You know, it was 100 women of us in one cattle wagon. It was crowded. Again, when-- we were-- once we were loaded, and they locked the doors-- the Nazis locked the doors-- I saw what was inside. There was one barrel, you know, for releasing yourself. And there were maybe two buckets of water. And this was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: No bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember if they gave us any food for the road.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was this journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: The journey take maybe 24 hours, two days. I don't remember. Because, you know, the distance from Plaszow to Auschwitz is rather short. However, you know, we were not going first class. So wherever they using the side tracks. So little by little, we did arrive in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever open the car?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first time we saw light when we arrived in Auschwitz. And over there, they were waiting already, the Nazis. Somebody mentioned that they saw Mengele in this group. I do not remember. I met Mengele on a later day in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we were brought to Auschwitz and they noticed a list with names, they were [? stumped. ?] They were not used to it. What does it mean, a list with names? So in order until they would find out what to do with us, the bureaucracy-- in the meantime, they took us to Birkenau. Who had the list?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was always one person who was in charge in our group.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Jew. And it was, I believe, one of the Nazis who was escorting us. When they saw it in Auschwitz, they-- they just did not know what to do. So the safest thing was to do, to bring us to Birkenau. And they kept us for the rest of the day in the front of the crematoria.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when the-- when the train-- when the doors opened on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- what happened? I can still hear the sound, when then Nazis gave orders, jump. Quickly get out from the cattle wagons. And right away, they sorted us. You know, the-- the only thing what was constantly what we experienced, that the 300 women were always kept separated. And when they took us-- the whole group-- and as I say, they did not know, because they were given the list of names. We are Schindler Juden. They did not know. So that's what I say. They took us in front of the crematorium, waiting for how to resolve the problem. So we, for the whole day, were in front of the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you any idea what that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, you know, it was elementary. Of course you knew what it is that you are-- because, standing in front of the ovens, and watching the black, black smoke, with the flames going to the sky and smelling the stench of burning flesh, you knew where you are. But then, towards the end of the day, they came to order. And we were brought to a barrack. In this barrack, we were ordered to undress completely, put everything on this side. And we were brought from this room, naked, in one next room, next barrack. And this was a shock that we will never recover from it, because in this barrack, they were the Nazi men in the uniforms. And there were even the-- the collaborators in the black uniforms. And they were there, who were shaving us, wherever a hair grows. The shock was beyond description. We did not know what was going on with us. And they were-- they were very cruel, the way they were shaving us. They had fun. But we were numb. We did not know what's going on with us. Until, after the shaving--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. Helen, who were the collaborators?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had in Poland, they had all kinds of collaborators. The Nazis found Poland an excellent ground to get help, as they were the Ukrainians. They were horrible. They were Polish collaborators, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were uniformed in black uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The Ukrainians wore the black uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were so shocked.

speaker: interviewee
text: You were numb. You were in such a shock. You did not know what's going on, because the next room was waiting another group of Nazis. And they were spraying us with some kind of disinfectant. And-- but we were numb. Number one, the most scary thing was, you know, not realizing, because those were not-- they cannot comprehend. You cannot imagine. You did not recognize your sister. You looked so different. We looked not human. We looked like creatures.

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally, after the spraying, we were brought to the shower room. Who can forget the sound when the steel doors closed, to the shower room? I mean, we are packed in the shower room. Nobody dared to take a breath, because all did-- we looked at the ceilings, looking at the vents and waiting for the Zyklon B to come down, the gasses. How did you know about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, you already heard what was going on, what the shower rooms were, for what purpose. We spent over there in the shower room a few anxious moment until, unexpectedly, they start the gas water came down. So for the first time, we took a deep breath, and again, with this sigh of relief and hoping, maybe we're gonna live another day and another day. This was the feeling that we are saved for a-- for a short time. And from there, we were brought-- from the shower room, we were brought to a barrack, in which there is no excuse for it. I can never forgive or understand how there [? not ?] over there were women working in this barrack. How, like sadists-- so sadistically, when they saw a tall woman, they would throw something, just a small piece of clothing, just barely to cover their body. And yet, when they saw a short woman, they would throw something warm and long, a-- a bigger garment. So we were only in the wooden shoes, bare feet, no socks, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were not uniforms, Helen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Over there, they gave us such rags, I would say. And we looked horrible. And from there, we march-- they marched us to a barrack. When we arrived in this barrack, always the 300 women separately, they brought us-- after two and a half days-- they brought us the black coffee. This was our first nourishment. And we thought-- we felt-- that not only what the Nazis did to us, but hopelessness entered, because you thought cannot survive. You cannot go on with life. What we experienced, short-- in such a short time enough, it's-- and this was very scary, sad, when you were losing hope. However, we went through plenty in Auschwitz. Dr. Mengele did selections and all kinds of things. Thank god, because the whole world knew what was going on. They knew very well. They did nothing. Schindler did not rest. He tried to do the impossible thing. He was sending people. He was, himself, intervening. He was determined in asking for his 300 women. It was not simple.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yet he was so determined that he succeeded, because after a few weeks in Auschwitz, there came the order. Again, they picked us over there. They gave us some food before we left. And were-- they were-- we were escorted, put in the cattle cars, locked again, and we traveled-- I don't remember, again, three days, four days-- and we finally arrived in Brunnlitz. However, I would like to make one-- yes? In Auschwitz, did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No. The 300 women were kept, you know, like animals, separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you always kept in the barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Of course, only for the roll call we had to step outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned that you saw Mengele?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Mengele came. And he-- once, twice, I-- I saw him. Number one-- he selected, one day, a few women, took them to a barrack, and they took blood from those women. But later on they were escorted back to our group.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were part of the Schindler Juden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And we were over there. He did, you know, some selections while we were counted the roll call in the morning. This was not, you know-- it sounds better than it was, because this was simply torture, to be almost naked. October, November-- it's bitter cold. And we had to be naked, stripped, only the wooden shoes on our feet. And we had to be at the roll call in front of the barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Always naked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we'll wait until the end of this tape, now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
